message:
fuse E2E: wrap python3 ioctl helper with /usr/bin/timeout for hard kill

The previous commit added a SIGALRM watchdog inside the Python helper
so MOVE_EXT fcntl.ioctl calls that stall in the kernel could surface a
structured timeout report. SIGALRM only works if Python keeps its own
signal handlers installed — when the kernel blocks the caller inside
the ioctl syscall the Python process can still hang indefinitely.
Belt-and-suspenders: wrap the python3 invocation with /usr/bin/timeout
20s so a second-level watchdog always returns control.

Behavior:
- If `timeout` is available on PATH, shell out as
  `timeout 20s python3 -c script ...`. Otherwise fall back to the bare
  python3 invocation (development environments without coreutils).
- When /usr/bin/timeout fires, it exits with status 124. Capture that
  exit code in the Rust helper and synthesize the same JSON envelope
  the Python-side watchdog produces — `{"timeout": true, "message":
  "move_ext ioctl timed out"}` — so the Rust test assertions that check
  `report["timeout"]` handle both watchdog paths identically.
- All other exit paths (success or Python-side OSError reporting)
  still assert on output.status.success() and keep the existing JSON
  contract.

This closes the last stall-loophole in the mounted-path MOVE_EXT E2E
tests without affecting local or CI environments where the ioctl
behaves normally.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
